description: Quest chains shown on the /onboarding page. Data-driven so new quests can be added without touching the page code. Progress is tracked per step in the user's localStorage under the key 'humanity_quest_progress'. Rewritten for v0.220+ to lead with self-sufficiency skills.

chains.title: First Steps
chains.description: Get set up in under five minutes. Your identity is created automatically when you open the chat.

steps.title: Say hello
steps.description: Open the chat, pick a display name, and introduce yourself in #welcome. Your cryptographic identity is created on your device automatically. No signup, no email, no password.

steps.title: Set up your profile
steps.description: Add a bio and let people know what skills you have or what you want to learn. Are you good with tools? Know about gardening? Interested in solar?

steps.title: Back up your seed phrase
steps.description: In Settings, write down your 24-word recovery phrase. This is the only way to recover your identity if you lose your device. Store it somewhere safe, offline.

steps.title: Create your first task
steps.description: Open the Tasks page and add something you want to accomplish this week. Could be as simple as 'research rain barrels' or 'price solar panels.'

chains.title: Water Independence
chains.description: 50,000 people in Lake Tahoe lost water access in 2025 because of wildfires. Learn to collect, filter, and store your own clean water so you never depend on a single point of failure.

steps.title: Calculate your water usage
steps.description: The average American uses 80-100 gallons per day. Write down what you actually use: drinking, cooking, cleaning, garden. Knowing your baseline is step one.

steps.title: Learn rainwater collection basics
steps.description: A 1,000 sq ft roof in an area with 30 inches of annual rainfall collects about 18,000 gallons per year. Check the Resources page for guides on collection systems.

steps.title: Understand filtration options
steps.description: From simple carbon filters ($20) to gravity-fed ceramic systems ($200) to full reverse osmosis ($300+). Match the system to your water source and budget.

steps.title: Plan your water storage
steps.description: Two weeks of emergency water for one person is about 14 gallons. A family of four needs 56 gallons minimum. IBC totes (275 gallons) are cheap and stackable.

chains.title: Food Independence
chains.description: Indoor aeroponic towers use 90% less water than soil farming, grow food year-round, and need no yard. The simulation teaches you the skills before you build the real thing.

steps.title: Calculate your food needs
steps.description: One person needs roughly 2,000 calories per day. A single aeroponic tower can produce 30+ heads of lettuce per month, plus herbs and small vegetables. Start with what you eat most.

steps.title: Try the farming simulation
steps.description: The in-app simulation lets you experiment with tower placement, lighting, nutrient schedules, and crop rotation without spending a dollar. Make your mistakes here first.

steps.title: Plan your first tower
steps.description: A basic DIY aeroponic tower costs $50-150 in materials. PVC pipe, net cups, a small pump, timer, and nutrients. Check the Resources page for build guides and parts lists.

steps.title: Learn about closed-loop systems
steps.description: Aquaponics combines fish farming with hydroponics. Fish waste feeds plants, plants clean the water. One system produces both protein and vegetables with minimal external inputs.

steps.title: Share your surplus
steps.description: Once your tower is producing more than you eat, post surplus on the Market page. Trade with neighbors. Build the local food network that makes everyone more resilient.

chains.title: Energy Independence
chains.description: When the grid goes down, everything else follows. Water pumps, refrigeration, communication, heating, cooling. Learn to generate and store your own power.

steps.title: Audit your energy consumption
steps.description: Check your electric bill. The average US household uses about 30 kWh per day. A kill-a-watt meter ($20) shows exactly where your power goes. Cut waste before you generate.

steps.title: Learn solar panel basics
steps.description: A single 400W panel costs $150-300 and produces about 1.6 kWh per day (assuming 4 peak sun hours). That is enough to run LED lights, charge devices, and power a small fridge.

steps.title: Understand battery storage
steps.description: Lithium iron phosphate (LiFePO4) batteries last 10+ years and handle thousands of charge cycles. A 5 kWh battery ($300-600) covers most essential overnight needs.

steps.title: Identify your essential circuits
steps.description: What absolutely must stay on? Fridge, water pump, lights, communication. Size your system for essentials first, then expand. A 2 kW system covers most critical needs.

chains.title: Build Your Network
chains.description: Self-sufficiency does not mean doing everything alone. The strongest communities are networks of people who each bring different skills and share freely.

steps.title: Find people near you
steps.description: Use the Maps page to find community gardens, maker spaces, tool libraries, and other people on HumanityOS in your area. Introduce yourself.

steps.title: Share what you know
steps.description: Everyone has something to teach. Plumbing, electrical, cooking, childcare, car repair, first aid. Add your skills to your profile so people can find you.

steps.title: Start a group project
steps.description: A community garden, a tool-sharing co-op, a neighborhood solar install, a bulk food buy. Use Tasks to coordinate, Chat to communicate, Maps to plan.

steps.title: Vouch for someone you trust
steps.description: When you vouch for someone, you are telling the network that this person is real and trustworthy. It helps everyone distinguish genuine people from fake accounts.

chains.title: Going Deeper
chains.description: When you are ready to do more, or help build the platform itself.

steps.title: Install the desktop app
steps.description: Native app that works offline. Same identity as the web version. Includes the 3D simulation engine for farming, building, and planning.

steps.title: 3D print real tools
steps.description: Models from the simulation are exportable for 3D printing. Design a hydroponics fitting in the sim, print it, install it. The game teaches skills for the real world.

steps.title: Run your own server
steps.description: One binary, under ten minutes from zero to live. Your community gets its own space. Users keep their identity when they move between servers.

steps.title: Help build HumanityOS
steps.description: Code, writing, design, translation, testing, feedback. This is a public-domain project. Everything built here belongs to everyone.

steps.title: Read the Humanity Accord
steps.description: The philosophical foundation. Defines the constraints every system must respect: dignity, consent, transparency, non-domination. Short and plain-language.
